title: Applying a silicone mastic bath seal
subtitle: Fill the joint between bath and wall tiles with waterproof silicone
excerpt: How to seal the joint between a bath and the tiled or painted wall above it using silicone mastic. Covers removing old sealant, masking for a straight bead, tooling the bead, and the cure time before the bath is used.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6093: Design of joints and jointing in building construction. CTDA (Ceramic Tile Distributors Association) guidance on wet-area sealing. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: A flexible, waterproof sealant based on silicone rubber, used to fill movement joints in bathrooms and kitchens. Unlike rigid grout, it accommodates the small movement between a bath or shower tray and the wall as the bath flexes under load. Available in sanitary grade (mould-resistant) for wet areas; standard grades should not be used in permanently damp locations.

The period during which silicone mastic cross-links and becomes fully waterproof. Sanitary silicone is typically touch-dry in 2 to 4 hours but does not achieve full waterproof cure for 24 hours. The bath must not be used during this period, as water contact before full cure prevents the silicone from bonding correctly to the tub surface.

content.text: The joint between a bath rim and the wall tiles flexes every time someone gets in or out of the bath. Grout cannot accommodate this movement and cracks within months.

is the correct material: it bonds to both surfaces and remains flexible. Sanitary-grade silicone contains a fungicide that resists the black mould that appears on standard silicone in damp rooms. A neat, well-tooled bead lasts 10 to 15 years. A thick or lumpy bead traps water at its edges and fails much sooner.

Sanitary-grade silicone mastic (matching the existing grout colour). A caulking gun (the silicone tube fits a standard gun). A utility knife. A silicone smoothing tool or a rounded plastic spoon. Masking tape (low-tack). Isopropyl alcohol or white spirit for cleaning. Paper towels. A bucket of warm water.

Old silicone must be fully removed before new silicone is applied; new silicone does not bond well to old. Cut along both edges of the old bead with the utility knife and peel it away. Work a filling knife along the seam to lever out any remaining pieces. Remove the last traces by rubbing with a silicone remover spray or with fine-grade abrasive (400-grit wet-and-dry) on the bath surface. The joint must be clean, dry, and free of any soap residue or old sealant before the new bead goes in.
Step 2, fill the bath before sealing

Fill the bath with cold water to its normal bath-water level before applying the silicone. An empty bath sits slightly higher than a full one: if you seal on an empty bath, the silicone stretches and pulls away from the wall every time the bath is used under load. Sealing with the bath filled at normal load keeps the joint at the size it will be in use.
Step 3, mask both sides of the joint

Apply two strips of masking tape: one along the bath surface 3 to 4 mm from the wall, and one along the wall (or tile face) 3 to 4 mm above the joint. The gap between the two strips is the width of the finished bead. Masking ensures clean edges and makes tooling easier. Press the tape edges down firmly so silicone cannot creep underneath.
Step 4, apply and tool the bead

Cut the nozzle of the silicone cartridge at 45 degrees to give a bead slightly larger than the joint gap. Load the tube into the gun and apply a continuous bead into the joint, moving at an even pace. Do not stop and restart mid-run: any restart creates a lump that is hard to smooth. Once the full run is done, wet a finger or a silicone-smoothing tool and drag it along the bead in one smooth pass, pressing the silicone into the joint and leaving a slightly concave surface. Remove the masking tape immediately (before the silicone skins) pulling at 45 degrees away from the bead.
Allow 24 hours before draining the bath or using the bath. Silicone is touch-dry in 2 to 4 hours but does not reach full waterproof
for a full day. Drain the bath only after the cure is complete, as the joint will stretch slightly as the bath rises.

attrs.symptom: Silicone peeling away from the bath rim within weeks
attrs.cause: Old silicone or soap scum was not fully removed before the new bead was applied.
attrs.fix: Remove all new silicone, clean the bath surface thoroughly with isopropyl alcohol, allow it to dry completely, and re-seal.

attrs.symptom: Black mould reappearing on the new sealant within months
attrs.cause: Standard silicone used instead of sanitary grade, or the bathroom is poorly ventilated.
attrs.fix: Use sanitary-grade silicone on the next replacement. Improve ventilation by fitting or running an extractor fan during and after baths.

attrs.symptom: Bead is lumpy and uneven at the tooled surface
attrs.cause: Finger or tool was dry when tooling; silicone dragged rather than sliding.
attrs.fix: Wet the tooling finger or tool thoroughly before each pass. A very small drop of washing-up liquid on the finger helps the silicone release cleanly.
